content.text: The most common reason a Cowork task produces a disappointing result is not a limitation of the engine — it is an underspecified goal. The engine will always do
something

, but without a clear outcome it is likely to do the wrong something. Before you write a task prompt, spend a moment answering one question: what would a perfect result look like, in concrete terms? The answer to that question is your outcome definition.
The Five Properties of an Actionable Outcome

attrs.alt: Anatomy of a well-defined Cowork outcome: five properties (Specific, Measurable, Scoped, Actionable, Output-Specified) all feeding into the central Goal Statement
attrs.title: The Five Properties of an Actionable Cowork Outcome

content.text: A goal statement that satisfies all five properties gives the agentic engine enough information to plan, execute, and decide when it is done — without guessing or asking clarifying questions mid-task.
Property 1: Specific

Specificity means naming a concrete deliverable, not an activity. The engine is not a collaborator you brief; it is a program that must select tools and produce output. Vague verbs — "help," "think about," "look into" — do not map to actions. Concrete nouns do.
Vague
Specific
What changed
Help me with the Q2 report
Write a 3-paragraph executive summary of the Q2 report
Named the artifact (executive summary) and its length
Analyse our customer feedback
Produce a list of the 10 most common complaint categories, ranked by frequency, from feedback.csv
Named the artifact (ranked list), the count (10), and the input
Review the codebase
List all functions in auth.py that lack input validation, with the line number and a one-line description of the risk
Named the criterion (missing input validation), the file, and the output structure
Property 2: Measurable

A measurable outcome tells the engine when it is done. Without a stopping condition, the engine will keep going — adding more items to a list, writing more sections to a report, digging deeper into data — until it hits a token limit or makes an arbitrary decision to stop. Measurability is most often expressed as a count, a threshold, or a format to fill.
Count-based stopping conditions

# Engine knows it is done when the list has 10 items
"List the 10 highest-impact code-quality issues in the repository"

# Engine stops after 3 scenarios
"Write 3 failure scenarios for the payment processing flow"

# Engine stops after one slide per section
"Create one summary bullet point per section of the annual report"
Threshold-based stopping conditions

# Engine stops when all items above the threshold are included
"List all expense line items above $5,000 in the Q2 budget"

# Engine stops when all files matching the criterion are covered
"Flag every function with cyclomatic complexity > 10 in src/"
Format-based stopping conditions
A template or schema acts as a natural stopping condition: the task is complete when every field in the template is populated.

For each contract in /workspace/contracts/, extract the following fields
and write them to /workspace/contract-data.csv:
  - contract_id
  - effective_date
  - expiry_date
  - governing_law
  - liability_cap (dollar amount or "unlimited")

The task is complete when every contract has a row in the CSV.
Property 3: Scoped

Scope defines the boundary of what the engine is allowed to look at. Without a scope, the engine interprets ambiguous instructions broadly — and often expensively. "Review the codebase" might cause it to read hundreds of files when you only wanted it to review one module.
Time scope
"Q2 2026" limits the engine to data from that quarter
"contracts signed in the last 90 days" filters by date
"commits since the v2.3.0 tag" scopes a code review to recent changes
File and data scope
"in /workspace/project-a/" limits the engine to that folder
"from feedback.csv" names the exact input file
"in the BACKEND Jira project" scopes a connector query
Topic scope
"security issues only" tells the engine to ignore style, performance, and correctness findings
"customer-facing features only" excludes internal tooling
"the payment module" limits a codebase review to one component
Under-scoped vs over-scoped

: an under-scoped task costs extra tokens and often produces superficial coverage across too many files or too much data. An over-scoped task may miss the relevant material entirely. Aim for the narrowest scope that captures everything you actually care about.
Property 4: Actionable

Actionability means the outcome is achievable with the tools the engine has available: the files in the workspace, the connectors you have authorized, and the operations those connectors support. An outcome that requires a tool the engine does not have will stall or produce a hallucinated result.
Check before you submit
What the task requires
What to verify
Reading a specific file
The file is in the workspace (uploaded or previously written)
Reading from Google Drive
The Drive connector is authorized and the folder is within the granted scope
Creating Jira tickets
The Jira connector is authorized and the project is accessible
Running code
The sandbox tool is available (depends on your Cowork plan)
Accessing a private internal system
Not actionable without a connector — export data to a file first
Property 5: Output-Specified

Specifying the output format and destination is the property most often omitted — and the one that causes the most friction in the handoff to other apps. The engine will choose a default format when none is given, and that default may not match where you intend to use the result.
Format
Markdown
(.md) — for Notion, GitHub, or Markdown-aware editors
CSV
— for Excel, Google Sheets, or further data processing
JSON
— for API consumption, configuration files, or downstream scripts
Plain text
— for email body, chat messages, or clients that do not render Markdown
Destination
Workspace path
: "write to /workspace/project-a/summary.md"
In the task response
: "reply with the result in the activity log" (for short outputs you will copy)
Connector destination
: "create a Google Doc in the Marketing/Reports folder"
Outcome vs Approach: What Not to Specify

Defining a good outcome does not mean specifying how the engine should achieve it. Over-constraining the approach is a common mistake that limits the engine's ability to find the most efficient solution.
Over-specified approach
Outcome-focused version
Why it matters
First read file A, then read file B, then compare them
Compare file A and file B and note all differences
Engine may read them more efficiently in parallel
Sort the rows by column C, then filter by column D > 100, then sum column E
From data.csv, sum column E for all rows where column D > 100
The intermediate sort step was unnecessary; the engine skips it
Use Python to count the words in each document
Count the words in each document in /workspace/docs/ and produce a CSV with filename, word_count
Engine can use any efficient method; Python isn't required

The exception: specify the approach when it is a genuine requirement, not just a preference. If a compliance policy requires data to be processed without external network calls, say so. If a house style mandates a specific citation format, include it. Constraints that are actually requirements belong in the goal definition.
Decomposing Ambiguous Goals

Some goals are genuinely too broad for one task. "Improve the quality of our documentation" is not a task — it is a project. The right technique is to decompose it into a set of concrete outcomes, each of which can be defined with all five properties.
Decomposition example: documentation quality
Starting goal:
Improve the quality of our API documentation.
Audit task
: "Read every .md file in /workspace/api-docs/ and produce a list of endpoints that are missing a usage example, with their file path and line number. Write to /workspace/missing-examples.csv."
Consistency task

: "Read /workspace/api-docs/style-guide.md and then read each .md file in /workspace/api-docs/. List every parameter description that does not follow the style guide's required format. Write to /workspace/style-violations.csv."
Example generation task
: "For each endpoint in /workspace/missing-examples.csv, write a curl usage example and insert it into the correct .md file, immediately after the Parameters section."
Each decomposed task is specific, measurable, scoped, actionable, and output-specified. The output of task 1 feeds task 3 as its input — a task chain through the workspace.
Quality Criteria in the Goal Statement
Beyond structure and format, you can embed quality criteria directly in the goal. These act as constraints the engine applies during generation, not as post-processing instructions.
Tone and audience

"Write for a non-technical executive audience — no jargon, no acronyms
 without a first-use definition, sentences no longer than 25 words."
Confidence threshold

"Only include findings you are confident about based on the data.
 If the evidence is ambiguous, omit the finding rather than guess."
Completeness requirement

"Every contract must have a row, even if some fields are blank.
 Use empty string for fields you cannot extract, not null or 'N/A'."

Quality criteria work because the engine applies them during the generation loop, not as an afterthought. Telling the engine "omit findings you are uncertain about" means it will not hallucinate uncertain findings to fill out a list — a common failure mode when no confidence criterion is given.
Diagnosing a Poorly Defined Goal
If a task produces an unexpected or low-quality result, one of the five properties was missing. Use this checklist to diagnose and rewrite the goal before resubmitting:
Symptom
Missing property
Fix
Output is too generic or high-level
Specific
Name the exact deliverable ("a ranked list of 10...")
Output is bloated — too many items, too many words
Measurable
Add a count or length constraint
Engine read irrelevant files or covered the wrong time period
Scoped
Add an explicit folder path, date range, or topic boundary
Engine said it could not access the required data
Actionable
Upload the file or authorize the connector before resubmitting
Output is in the wrong format or in the wrong place
Output-Specified
Add "write to /workspace/... as CSV" or "reply in the activity log"
Iterating on a Goal: Refinement Patterns

Goals rarely land correctly on the first draft. Cowork tasks support an edit-and-rerun workflow: cancel a running task, revise the goal statement, then resubmit. Three refinement patterns cover most cases.
Tighten scope when the output is too broad
Draft goal
Problem
Refined goal
Summarize the Q4 pipeline run logs
Logs span 14 systems; output will be unmanageable
Summarize error-level log lines from the ingestion service only, grouped by error code, as a markdown table
Audit the codebase for security issues
No scope boundary; could run indefinitely
Check the src/api/ directory for SQL injection and missing input validation; output a numbered list with file and line references
Add a stopping condition when the task runs open-ended
If a task times out or keeps asking clarifying questions, the goal is missing a natural stopping point. Add a count limit, a time boundary, or an explicit definition of done.

# Before — open-ended
Research competitors in the HR software space.

# After — bounded
Research 5 competitors in the HR software space. For each, list:
- Pricing tier structure (if public)
- Primary customer segment
- One differentiating feature
Output as a markdown table. Stop at 5 companies.
Split compound goals when the task stalls mid-way

A task that requires judgment at each step is not a single automated task — it is a workflow. If Cowork stops to ask what to do next, the goal contained a branch that needs human input. Split it into two sequential tasks with an explicit handoff point.

# Compound (problematic)
Audit the meeting notes from last quarter,
flag action items that are past due,
then draft follow-up messages for each one
and send them to the relevant Slack channels.

# Split task 1
Read the meeting notes in /workspace/q4-notes/.
List every action item with its due date and owner.
Output as a CSV with columns: item, due_date, owner.

# [Human reviews CSV, confirms it, starts task 2]

# Split task 2
For each row in action-items.csv where due_date < today,
draft a follow-up Slack message to the owner.
Save each draft as a separate .txt file in /workspace/drafts/.
Do not send; output a list of files created.
Multi-Step Tasks and Intermediate Outcomes

Long workflows contain multiple outcomes — one for each stage. Defining intermediate outcomes is as important as defining the final one, because Cowork uses each stage’s output as the input to the next.
Stage 1 outcome:
a structured artifact (CSV, JSON, markdown table) that is machine-readable and self-contained.
Stage 2 outcome:
a transformed version of that artifact, or a new artifact that references it by filename.
Final outcome:
the deliverable the end user will read or act on — a report, an email draft, a Slack message, a pull request.
The key rule: each intermediate outcome must be a file in the workspace, not a conversational response. Cowork tasks cannot reliably pass prose from one run to the next — they pass files.
Design for files. Every stage output should land in /workspace/ as a named file with a stable path. Subsequent tasks reference the path, not the content.
Common Goal-Definition Mistakes
These five mistakes account for the majority of Cowork task failures or mediocre outputs.
Mistake
Symptom
Fix
Activity framing
Task runs but produces unexpected output format
Rewrite using noun deliverables, not verb activities
Missing output format
Output is a prose paragraph when you needed a table
Add ‘Output as [format]’ to the goal statement
Overloaded goal
Task stops mid-way with clarifying questions
Split into two sequential tasks with a workspace handoff
No stopping condition
Task times out or loops on the same source material
Add a count, date range, or explicit done condition
Implicit assumptions
Output omits things you assumed would be included
List required fields or columns explicitly in the goal
Summary

A well-defined outcome is the single most effective way to improve Cowork task quality. The five properties — Specific, Measurable, Scoped, Actionable, and Output-Specified — give the engine what it needs to plan, execute, and decide when it is done. Specify the
what
and the
how good
; let the engine decide the
how

. When a task goal is too broad, decompose it into a chain of specific tasks linked by workspace files. When a task produces a poor result, diagnose which property was missing and rewrite the goal before resubmitting.
